_meta.authoringNotes: Pathophysiology and classification per Eichenholtz, Sanders & Frykberg, and Rogers et al. Diabetes Care 2011 (PMID 21868781) Joint Charcot consensus. IWGDF 2023 guidance (PMID 36511079) for diagnosis and treatment. ADA Standards 2025 for foot care. Mortality data per Sohn et al. Diabetes Care 2009 (PMID 19628812). Total contact cast outcomes per Pinzur et al. 2007 and de Souza et al. 2010.

h1Title: Charcot Foot: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Charcot foot (Charcot neuroarthropathy) is a progressive bone and joint destruction of the insensate foot that can collapse the midfoot arch within weeks, most commonly as a complication of diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Annual incidence is roughly 0.3% in diabetes overall and 8.5 per 1,000 in patients with established neuropathy. The acute phase presents as a unilaterally red, hot, swollen foot in a patient who can usually still walk on it, which is the clinical sign that distinguishes it from cellulitis. Untreated, ongoing weight-bearing produces the classic rocker-bottom deformity, midfoot ulceration, osteomyelitis, and a 5-year amputation rate of 28-49%. Early diagnosis and immediate offloading with a total contact cast for 8-16 weeks prevent deformity in over 80% of patients and reduce later ulceration by more than half.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime risk 0.1-0.4% in unselected diabetes; 8.5 per 1,000 person-years in patients with peripheral neuropathy
keyStats.demographics: Diabetic patients account for over 75% of cases; mean age 50-60 years; affects men and women roughly equally
keyStats.avgAge: Typical onset between 40 and 65 years, usually 10-20 years after diabetes diagnosis
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 250,000-600,000 affected adults globally based on the 537 million adults with diabetes (IDF 2024)

definition: Charcot foot (ICD-10: M14.671 right foot; M14.672 left foot) is a non-infectious destructive arthropathy of the foot and ankle that occurs in patients with peripheral neuropathy. The current pathogenic model combines two complementary theories: the neurotraumatic model (repetitive painless microtrauma in an insensate foot accumulates without protective response) and the neurovascular model (autonomic neuropathy causes increased blood flow that triggers osteoclast activation, RANK-ligand release, and accelerated bone resorption). The result is fragmentation, dislocation, and remodeling of midfoot, hindfoot, or ankle joints over weeks to months. Diabetes mellitus accounts for over 75% of contemporary cases; other neuropathies (alcoholic, syphilitic, leprotic, post-traumatic, hereditary sensory and autonomic neuropathy) can also cause it. The disease is classified by anatomic location (Brodsky type 1 midfoot, the most common pattern, accounts for 60-70% of cases) and by clinical-radiographic stage (Eichenholtz 0 prodromal, 1 fragmentation, 2 coalescence, 3 reconstruction). Charcot foot is managed by a coordinated team of podiatry, endocrinology, vascular surgery, infectious disease, orthotists, and orthopedic foot-and-ankle surgery, with the central principle being immediate and prolonged offloading.

typesClassification.description: The most common location, accounting for 60-70% of cases. Produces collapse of the medial longitudinal arch and the classic rocker-bottom deformity. Plantar midfoot ulceration over a bony prominence is the typical complication.
Approximately 15-20% of cases. Produces hindfoot instability and ankle malalignment. Often slower to consolidate and more likely to require operative stabilization.
Approximately 10% of cases. Ankle Charcot is the least stable form and most likely to fail conservative management; surgical fusion is common.
Avulsion or fragmentation of the posterior calcaneus, often initiated by Achilles tightness. Causes posterior heel pad ulceration.

typesClassification.type: Brodsky type 4 — Multiple regions; Type 5 — Forefoot
typesClassification.description: Type 4 involves multiple regions simultaneously. Type 5, isolated forefoot involvement, is uncommon and may follow surgery or trauma.

primarySymptoms: Unilateral foot or ankle redness, warmth, and swelling — the affected foot is typically 2-7 °C warmer than the contralateral side on infrared thermography.
Mild aching or discomfort that is markedly less than the visible inflammation suggests; pain is absent in 30-50% of cases because of the underlying neuropathy.
Foot deformity developing over weeks: collapse of the medial longitudinal arch, lateral protrusion of the cuboid, or rocker-bottom plantar contour.
A palpable bony prominence under the midfoot once the arch has collapsed, often the cuboid or navicular.
Continued ability to walk on the foot despite obvious swelling — a feature that distinguishes Charcot foot from acute infection in most cases.
Difficulty fitting normal footwear because of swelling and changing shape.
New foot ulceration over the plantar midfoot or lateral border once deformity is established.
Foot fatigue and asymmetric gait, particularly during late phases as the contralateral limb compensates.
Spontaneous fracture or dislocation noted on radiograph after minor trauma.
Loss of protective sensation on monofilament testing or vibration testing — the prerequisite neuropathy.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent unilateral foot warmth more than 2 °C warmer than the contralateral foot in a neuropathic patient
Unexplained foot swelling lasting more than a few days without pain or trauma
New shoe fit difficulty in one foot only
Failure of swelling to settle with overnight rest and elevation in a patient with diabetes
Mild foot discomfort with disproportionate visible erythema and swelling
emergencySigns: Sudden severe foot pain with rapidly progressive swelling and systemic features (fever, tachycardia) — exclude osteomyelitis or septic arthritis
Foot ulceration with surrounding cellulitis, purulent discharge, or visible bone
Cold, pale, pulseless limb suggesting acute critical limb ischemia
Hyperglycemia with ketones or new confusion in a diabetic patient with Charcot foot — diabetic emergency may coexist
Rapidly progressing deformity over days rather than weeks

causes.description: The dominant cause in over 75% of contemporary cases. Long-standing hyperglycemia damages small sensory and autonomic fibers, removing pain protection and altering bone vascular regulation. Most patients have had diabetes for more than 10 years and demonstrate loss of protective sensation on 10 g monofilament testing.

causes.cause: Autonomic neuropathy with increased blood flow
causes.description: Loss of vasomotor tone produces arteriovenous shunting and persistently increased blood flow to the foot. Increased perfusion drives osteoclast activation and accelerated bone turnover via the RANK/RANK-ligand/osteoprotegerin pathway, predisposing to fragmentation.

Normal weight-bearing produces small joint and ligamentous injuries that, in an insensate foot, accumulate without the patient adjusting gait or seeking rest. Continued walking on a microfracture is the proximate cause of rapid joint destruction.

Alcoholic neuropathy, tabes dorsalis (tertiary syphilis), leprosy, syringomyelia, Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, hereditary sensory and autonomic neuropathy, and post-spinal-cord-injury syndromes can cause identical pathology in non-diabetic populations.

Minor trauma in the neuropathic foot triggers release of TNF-alpha, IL-1, and IL-6, which up-regulate RANK-ligand and increase osteoclast activity. This molecular cascade explains why offloading alone often arrests destruction by interrupting the inflammatory feedback loop.
Surgical or accidental trauma can precipitate acute Charcot foot in a previously stable neuropathic patient. Post-operative immobilization may mask the early presentation and delay diagnosis.

riskFactors.description: The strongest single risk factor. Annual incidence among diabetic patients with neuropathy is 8.5 per 1,000 person-years versus under 1 per 1,000 without neuropathy. Loss of vibration sense and 10 g monofilament sensation are the most predictive features.
Charcot foot is rare in the first decade of diabetes; risk rises with each decade thereafter, mirroring the development of neuropathy and microvascular disease.

Higher HbA1c accelerates neuropathy and microvascular damage. Tight glycemic control reduces neuropathy progression by approximately 60% in type 1 diabetes (DCCT) and 25-30% in type 2 diabetes (UKPDS).
Obesity increases plantar loading and the magnitude of microtrauma. BMI over 30 roughly doubles risk in some cohorts; weight loss is part of long-term management.
Renal osteodystrophy and uremia accelerate bone turnover and worsen neuropathy. Patients on hemodialysis have 2-3 fold higher Charcot incidence.
Previous foot trauma identifies the highest-risk patients. Up to 25% of patients with one Charcot episode will develop bilateral disease over time.

Counterintuitive but well-documented: rapid improvement in glycemia and weight gain after transplant accelerates bone turnover and triggers Charcot in 10-20% of transplant recipients with pre-existing neuropathy.

affectedDemographics: Diabetic patients account for over 75% of contemporary cases (Sohn et al. Diabetes Care 2009)
Mean age at diagnosis 50-60 years, typically 10-20 years after diabetes onset
Men and women affected approximately equally in most contemporary diabetic cohorts
African American and Hispanic patients in US registries have moderately higher rates, paralleling diabetes prevalence
Less than 5% of cases occur in adults under 30 without secondary causes (juvenile diabetes with poor control, hereditary neuropathies)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is primarily clinical and time-critical: the acute red hot foot in a neuropathic patient is Charcot foot until proven otherwise, and immediate offloading is the highest priority even before imaging confirms the diagnosis. History captures duration of swelling, recent trauma (often minor or unremembered), foot ulcers, and diabetes control. Examination compares the temperature of both feet (infrared thermometer; difference greater than 2 °C is significant), looks for redness, swelling, deformity, and any breach of skin, and quantifies neuropathy with 10 g monofilament, 128 Hz tuning fork, and ankle reflexes. Vascular assessment with pedal pulses and ankle-brachial index excludes ischemia. Plain radiographs of both feet (weight-bearing AP, lateral, oblique) are first-line and may show subluxation, fragmentation, or fracture; up to 30% of cases are radiographically normal in the early acute phase. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is the most sensitive test for early disease, demonstrating subchondral bone marrow edema, subluxation, and joint effusion; differentiating Charcot from osteomyelitis can be difficult and may require combined nuclear imaging (white-cell-labeled SPECT or 18F-FDG PET) plus bone biopsy when an ulcer is present. Inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, white-cell count) are typically normal or mildly elevated in pure Charcot foot; substantial elevation should raise concern for superimposed infection. Pedal pulses and ankle-brachial index identify peripheral arterial disease that may need vascular workup.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Compares temperature of both feet at matched anatomic sites; a difference greater than 2 °C supports active Charcot in the acute phase and tracks resolution during offloading
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Quick bedside measurement at multiple sites on the dorsum and plantar surface; repeated at each clinic visit. Painless and immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging shows subluxation, fragmentation, fractures, and deformity; may be normal in early disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standing or simulated weight-bearing X-rays of both feet. Takes 10 minutes; results available same day.

diagnosticTests.test: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the foot
diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive test for early Charcot, identifying subchondral edema and microfractures invisible on X-ray; also helps distinguish Charcot from osteomyelitis when an ulcer is absent
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the scanner. Contrast may be used to assess soft tissue. Report available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: 10 g monofilament and 128 Hz vibration testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms protective sensation loss, the prerequisite for diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless bedside test of 5-10 sites on each foot. Inability to feel the monofilament at 4 or more sites confirms loss of protective sensation.

diagnosticTests.test: Inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, full blood count)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Mild elevation supports Charcot; marked elevation raises concern for superimposed infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results same day. Comparison to baseline values is more informative than single results in chronic kidney disease or hemoglobinopathy.

diagnosticTests.test: Combined SPECT/CT or 18F-FDG PET with bone biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates Charcot from osteomyelitis when an ulcer is present
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Imaging takes 2-3 hours. Bone biopsy is performed in the operating theater or interventional radiology with image guidance; results return in 3-5 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment has three sequential goals: immediate inflammation control with offloading, gradual return to weight-bearing in protective footwear, and prevention of recurrence. Diagnosis triggers total non-weight-bearing or partial weight-bearing in a total contact cast (TCC), which redistributes plantar pressure and immobilizes the affected joints. The TCC is changed every 1-2 weeks initially and continued until temperature symmetry and radiographic consolidation are achieved — typically 8-16 weeks but sometimes 6 months or longer. Removable cast walkers (CROW boot) are alternatives when patient adherence is high and skin integrity allows. Bisphosphonates (intravenous pamidronate 90 mg or zoledronate 5 mg) and calcitonin have been studied but are not recommended in current International Working Group on the Diabetic Foot (IWGDF 2023) guidance because randomized data show no functional benefit. Surgery is reserved for unstable, severe deformity not amenable to bracing (Eichenholtz stage 3 with rocker-bottom and recurrent ulceration), persistent instability of the ankle Charcot, and chronic osteomyelitis. Surgical options include exostectomy (removal of plantar bony prominence to allow ulcer healing), midfoot or ankle arthrodesis with rigid internal or external fixation, and reconstruction with combined plate, screw, and beaming techniques. Diabetes control is intensified throughout treatment with a target HbA1c of 7-7.5%; smoking cessation, weight optimization, and vascular intervention complete the package. After consolidation, lifelong protective footwear with custom-molded insoles and rigid rocker-bottom outsoles is mandatory, alongside daily skin inspection and 4-12 weekly podiatry review.

medicalTreatments.name: Total contact cast (TCC) with non-weight-bearing crutches or knee scooter
medicalTreatments.description: Gold-standard offloading. The cast distributes plantar pressure over the whole foot and lower leg, immobilizes the destructive joints, and reduces edema. Changed every 1-2 weeks for skin inspection. Continued until thermal symmetry and radiographic consolidation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Halts progression and prevents deformity in 80-95% of patients diagnosed in stage 0-1 disease

medicalTreatments.name: Charcot Restraint Orthotic Walker (CROW) or removable cast walker
medicalTreatments.description: Removable alternative to TCC; allows skin inspection. Less reliable in non-adherent patients, but useful when TCC application is impractical (insufficient access to skilled casting, fragile skin).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable consolidation rates to TCC when adherence is high; 60-75% in mixed populations

medicalTreatments.name: Intensive diabetes optimization
medicalTreatments.description: Target HbA1c 7-7.5% with insulin or GLP-1 receptor agonist. Reduces ongoing neuropathy progression and supports bone healing. Coordinated with diabetes specialist nursing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: DCCT and UKPDS data show 25-60% reduction in neuropathy progression with intensive glycemic control

medicalTreatments.name: Custom prescription footwear and orthotic insoles
medicalTreatments.description: Long-term maintenance after consolidation. Includes rigid rocker-bottom sole, extra-depth toe box, and total-contact molded insoles. Replaced every 6-12 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrent ulceration risk by 50-70% compared with retail footwear

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic therapy for superimposed infection
medicalTreatments.description: Targeted at culture-proven organisms in patients with ulceration or osteomyelitis. Empirical regimens cover Staphylococcus aureus and streptococci with consideration of Pseudomonas in chronic wounds.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiologic clearance in 70-85% of soft-tissue infection; bone infection often requires 6 weeks of intravenous and oral therapy plus surgical debridement

surgicalOptions.name: Exostectomy (resection of bony prominence)
surgicalOptions.description: Removes the protruding bone causing recurrent plantar ulceration in stable deformity. Outpatient or short-stay surgery; weight-bearing in TCC for 6-8 weeks afterward.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Ulcer healing in 70-85% of selected patients; recurrence within 2 years in 20-30%

surgicalOptions.name: Midfoot arthrodesis with plate, screws, or intramedullary bolts (beaming)
surgicalOptions.description: Fusion of unstable midfoot joints to restore plantigrade foot. Often combined with deformity correction. Hardware infection rates are higher than in non-diabetic surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Solid fusion in 75-90% of cases at 12 months; limb salvage at 5 years 80-90% in specialist centers

surgicalOptions.name: Ankle and hindfoot arthrodesis with retrograde intramedullary nail or external fixation
surgicalOptions.description: Used for unstable ankle Charcot (Brodsky type 3A) failing conservative management. Long postoperative immobilization (12-16 weeks); external fixation in patients with poor skin or infection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Limb salvage 70-90% at 5 years; reoperation rate 15-25%

surgicalOptions.name: Below-knee amputation
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for unsalvageable infection, intractable ulceration, or vascular insufficiency. Modern prosthetic rehabilitation allows independent mobility in over 60% of well-selected patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Wound healing in 70-85%; 5-year mortality 40-60% reflects underlying comorbidity rather than the amputation itself

alternativeTreatments.name: Custom-molded foot orthotics and pressure mapping
alternativeTreatments.description: Use of plantar pressure measurement (in-shoe sensors) to design orthotics that offload high-pressure zones. Reduces re-ulceration after consolidation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation and vascular risk modification
alternativeTreatments.description: Critical for tissue healing and long-term limb salvage. Combined nicotine replacement, bupropion, or varenicline plus structured behavioural support roughly doubles 12-month abstinence rates.

whySeeSpecialist: Charcot foot has a narrow window for non-operative success. Any neuropathic patient with a new unilateral red, hot, swollen foot should be referred to a multidisciplinary diabetic foot clinic within 24-48 hours. Delays beyond two weeks are strongly associated with permanent deformity, ulceration, and amputation. Joint care from podiatry, endocrinology, orthopedic foot-and-ankle surgery, and orthotics is the standard.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a podiatrist or orthopedic foot and ankle surgeon affiliated with a multidisciplinary diabetic foot clinic that performs at least 50 total contact casts per year. Surgical reconstruction should be performed in a center with experience in midfoot and ankle arthrodesis and access to vascular and infectious disease services.
hospitalCriteria: Multidisciplinary diabetic foot clinic (podiatry, endocrinology, vascular surgery, orthopedics, infectious disease)
Capability for total contact casting with on-site casting technicians
Pedal MRI and nuclear imaging available
Microbiology service for deep tissue and bone cultures
Orthotics and prosthetics service for custom footwear
keyFacilities: Tertiary orthopedic foot and ankle units
preventionStrategies: Maintain HbA1c at 7-7.5% to slow neuropathy progression.
Inspect both feet daily for warmth, swelling, redness, blisters, and ulceration; use a mirror to view the soles.
Wear well-fitted protective footwear with cushioned insoles at all times, including indoors.
Attend annual diabetic foot screening to detect early neuropathy and vascular disease.
Avoid walking on uneven surfaces, climbing ladders, and barefoot beach activities once neuropathy is established.
Stop smoking and treat hypertension and dyslipidemia to preserve foot circulation.
lifestyleModifications: Use offloading equipment (TCC, CROW boot, crutches, knee scooter) exactly as prescribed, including indoors and on short trips.
Sleep with the foot elevated to reduce edema in the first 2-4 weeks.
Hydrate the contralateral foot daily with emollient (urea-based cream) to prevent fissures.
Avoid weight-bearing exercise; substitute with upper-body strengthening and pool-based activity once skin allows.
Carry a foot-care emergency card listing podiatrist, endocrinologist, and emergency contacts.
Limit alcohol to under 14 units per week (under 7 in the presence of liver or pancreatic disease).

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet with whole grains, legumes, vegetables, oily fish, and olive oil to support glycemic control
Calcium 1,000-1,200 mg/day and vitamin D 800-1000 IU/day to support bone healing
Adequate protein (1-1.2 g/kg/day) during the acute phase to support fracture healing
Hydration with at least 1.5-2 L of fluid daily unless restricted
avoid: Sugar-sweetened beverages and refined carbohydrates that destabilize glycemia
High-sodium processed foods that aggravate edema
Foods that interfere with prescribed bisphosphonates if used (calcium-containing meals within 2 hours of oral bisphosphonate)

exerciseGuidelines: Weight-bearing exercise is avoided during the active phase. Pool-based exercise (when skin is intact), seated cycling, and upper-body resistance training maintain cardiovascular fitness without loading the foot. Once consolidated, gradual return to walking in protective footwear under podiatry supervision over 6-8 weeks.
dailyManagement: Inspect both feet at the same time every day, ideally before bed.
Measure foot temperature with an infrared thermometer if provided; report differences greater than 2 °C between feet.
Check blood glucose 2-4 times daily during the acute phase to maintain HbA1c targets.
Wear the TCC, CROW boot, or prescribed footwear consistently — even removing it briefly to walk to the bathroom can perpetuate the inflammatory cycle.
Take prescribed medications, including any antibiotic course, at the same times daily.
Attend follow-up podiatry, diabetic foot, and vascular appointments as scheduled.
prognosis: Outcomes depend on the speed of diagnosis and adherence to offloading. Diagnosed in the prodromal (Eichenholtz 0) or fragmentation (stage 1) phase and treated with immediate offloading, 80-95% of patients consolidate without permanent deformity within 4-12 months. Delayed presentation with established rocker-bottom deformity, midfoot ulceration, or osteomyelitis carries far worse outcomes: 5-year amputation rates of 28-49% in US Veterans Affairs data, and 5-year mortality from any cause of 28-44% reflecting underlying cardiovascular and renal comorbidities rather than the Charcot foot itself. Bilateral Charcot develops in up to 25% of patients within 10 years; lifelong custom footwear and quarterly podiatry review reduce recurrent ulceration by approximately 50%. Surgical reconstruction in specialist centers achieves limb salvage in 70-90% of unstable cases at 5 years.
recoveryTimeline: Acute phase offloading typically continues for 8-16 weeks until thermal symmetry returns. Transition to a CROW boot and then to custom protective footwear adds another 3-6 months. Full consolidation on imaging may take 6-12 months. Lifelong custom footwear is mandatory after consolidation.
complications: Plantar midfoot or hindfoot ulceration over bony prominence in 30-50% of patients with established deformity; daily skin inspection and accommodative footwear detect early changes.
Osteomyelitis in 20-30% of patients with chronic ulceration; identification combines clinical signs, probe-to-bone test, MRI, and bone biopsy.
Cellulitis and deep soft tissue infection; requires culture-directed antibiotics and sometimes surgical debridement.
Lower limb amputation in 5-20% over 5 years, dominated by failure of foot salvage in patients with combined Charcot, ulcer, and ischemia.
Contralateral Charcot foot in up to 25% over 10 years; mandates protective offloading of both feet during the acute phase.
Increased all-cause mortality from cardiovascular disease and chronic kidney disease, reflecting comorbidity rather than direct Charcot effect.

supportResources.name: American Diabetes Association — Foot Care
supportResources.description: Patient education on diabetic foot complications including Charcot foot and prevention strategies.

supportResources.name: International Working Group on the Diabetic Foot (IWGDF) 2023 guidelines
supportResources.description: Authoritative clinical guidelines for diagnosis, classification, and management of Charcot foot.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Foot and Ankle Society
supportResources.description: Patient information from the leading US orthopedic foot and ankle society.

supportResources.name: Diabetes UK — Foot care
supportResources.description: Patient-focused guide on foot complications, ulcer prevention, and accessing UK diabetic foot services.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetic Foot Ulcer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diabetic foot ulcer is a skin and soft tissue breach, often over pressure points, that can complicate Charcot foot or occur in isolation. Charcot foot is destruction of bone and joint with characteristic warmth and swelling; the two frequently coexist and require coordinated management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Type 2 diabetes is the underlying systemic disease that produces neuropathy and predisposes to Charcot foot. Charcot foot is a focal mechanical and inflammatory complication; treatment of Charcot does not control diabetes, and vice versa.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gout causes acute red, hot, swollen joints, most commonly the first metatarsophalangeal joint. Charcot foot affects multiple joints simultaneously, particularly the midfoot, in a neuropathic patient. Serum uric acid is elevated in gout; synovial fluid aspirate shows urate crystals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Osteoarthritis produces gradual joint pain and stiffness over years, with mild warmth at most. Charcot foot evolves over weeks with marked warmth, swelling, and bone destruction. Imaging in osteoarthritis shows joint-space narrowing and osteophytes rather than fragmentation.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic Foot Ulcer
relatedConditions.relevance: Complicates 30-50% of established Charcot deformities and shares the underlying neuropathy and vascular risk profile.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Underlying cause in over 75% of contemporary cases; controlling diabetes slows progression and reduces recurrence.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Accelerated bone turnover and corticosteroid exposure during co-morbidity management increase fracture risk; bone-protective therapy may be co-prescribed.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Underlying neuropathy is shared; some patients with Charcot foot also experience burning neuropathic pain in the contralateral limb.

faqs.question: What is Charcot foot in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Charcot foot is rapid breakdown of bones and joints in the foot in people whose nerve damage stops them from feeling pain. The foot becomes red, hot, and swollen and can collapse out of shape within weeks. Most cases occur in patients with long-standing diabetes.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of Charcot foot?
faqs.answer: The earliest signs are one foot that is warm, red, and swollen compared with the other, in a patient with neuropathy. Pain is usually mild and sometimes absent. Walking on the foot continues, but the shape begins to change over a few weeks.

faqs.question: What causes Charcot foot?
faqs.answer: Charcot foot is caused by peripheral neuropathy — most often from long-standing diabetes — combined with repetitive painless microtrauma and an inflammatory cascade that accelerates bone breakdown. Less commonly, alcoholic neuropathy, leprosy, syphilis, and hereditary neuropathies are responsible.

faqs.question: How is Charcot foot diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: a unilateral red, hot, swollen foot in a neuropathic patient. Confirmation comes from weight-bearing X-rays and MRI, which show subluxation, fragmentation, and bone marrow edema. Inflammatory markers and bone biopsy help distinguish Charcot from osteomyelitis when an ulcer is present.

faqs.question: How is Charcot foot treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is offloading with a total contact cast for 8-16 weeks until temperature symmetry and radiographic consolidation are achieved, followed by a CROW boot or custom protective footwear. Surgery is reserved for unstable severe deformity, recurrent ulceration, and chronic osteomyelitis.

faqs.question: Can Charcot foot be reversed?
faqs.answer: Caught in the prodromal or fragmentation phase and treated with immediate offloading, 80-95% of patients consolidate without permanent deformity. Established rocker-bottom deformity does not fully reverse but can be stabilized and the foot can be salvaged with bracing or surgery.

faqs.question: How long does Charcot foot take to heal?
faqs.answer: The acute phase typically resolves over 8-16 weeks of offloading, but full consolidation on imaging often takes 6-12 months. Transition from total contact cast to protective footwear is gradual. Lifelong custom footwear is required to prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: Is Charcot foot the same as a diabetic foot ulcer?
faqs.answer: No. A diabetic foot ulcer is a skin and soft tissue wound, while Charcot foot is destruction of the underlying bones and joints. The two frequently coexist because Charcot deformity creates pressure points where ulcers form; treatment requires addressing both.

faqs.question: Does Charcot foot always lead to amputation?
faqs.answer: Not at all. Most patients diagnosed early avoid amputation with offloading and protective footwear. Five-year amputation rates of 28-49% reported in older studies reflect late presentation; modern multidisciplinary clinics report limb salvage above 80% in specialist cohorts.

faqs.question: Should I keep walking on a Charcot foot?
faqs.answer: No. Continued weight-bearing perpetuates the inflammatory cycle and accelerates bone destruction. Strict offloading with total contact cast and crutches or a knee scooter is essential. Even short barefoot trips to the bathroom can delay healing.

faqs.question: Can Charcot foot affect both feet?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bilateral Charcot foot develops in up to 25% of patients over 10 years. Both feet should be protected when one is affected because compensatory weight-bearing on the unaffected side accelerates microtrauma in a neuropathic foot.

faqs.question: Is Charcot foot painful?
faqs.answer: Pain is mild or absent in 30-50% of patients because the neuropathy that predisposes to Charcot also blocks pain perception. This lack of pain is dangerous because patients continue to walk on the foot, accelerating destruction. Mild aching out of proportion to the visible swelling is typical.

faqs.question: What is a total contact cast?
faqs.answer: A total contact cast is a fiberglass or plaster cast molded to the entire shape of the lower leg and foot. It redistributes plantar pressure, immobilizes destructive joints, and is the gold-standard offloading device for acute Charcot foot. Casts are changed every 1-2 weeks.

faqs.question: Do bisphosphonates help in Charcot foot?
faqs.answer: Bisphosphonates were widely studied to reduce bone turnover but randomized trials have not shown functional benefit. Current IWGDF 2023 guidance does not recommend routine bisphosphonate use for Charcot foot. Offloading remains the only intervention with strong evidence.

faqs.question: How can I prevent Charcot foot?
faqs.answer: Maintain HbA1c at 7-7.5%, attend annual diabetic foot screening, wear well-fitted protective footwear, inspect both feet daily, and seek urgent review for any unilateral warmth, swelling, or redness. Stopping smoking and controlling blood pressure and cholesterol preserve foot circulation.

faqs.question: What does a Charcot foot look like later?
faqs.answer: Once collapsed, the midfoot loses its normal arch and develops a rocker-bottom shape with the cuboid or navicular bulging downward. Lateral overhang of the foot is common. Plantar ulcers form over bony prominences. Without protective footwear, recurrent ulceration leads to deeper infections.

faqs.question: When is surgery considered for Charcot foot?
faqs.answer: Surgery is considered for unstable deformity not controllable in a brace, recurrent ulceration over bony prominences, ankle Charcot failing conservative management, and chronic osteomyelitis. Options include exostectomy, midfoot or ankle fusion, and external fixation in selected patients.

faqs.question: Can transplant patients develop Charcot foot?
faqs.answer: Yes. Successful pancreas or kidney transplantation in patients with pre-existing diabetic neuropathy triggers Charcot foot in 10-20% of recipients, paradoxically because rapid improvement in glycemia and weight gain accelerates bone turnover. Routine podiatry surveillance is recommended post-transplant.

faqs.question: How do I know if my Charcot foot has healed?
faqs.answer: Healing is confirmed when temperature symmetry returns to within 2 °C of the contralateral foot at all sites and serial radiographs show consolidation without further fragmentation. This typically takes 4-6 months. The cast is changed to a CROW boot and then to custom protective footwear.

faqs.question: Will I need special shoes for life?
faqs.answer: Yes. Custom-molded insoles inside extra-depth shoes with rigid rocker-bottom outsoles are mandatory long-term to redistribute pressure and prevent ulceration. Standard retail footwear is associated with markedly higher recurrence; custom footwear is renewed every 6-12 months.

faqs.question: Is Charcot foot only seen in diabetes?
faqs.answer: No, but diabetes accounts for over 75% of contemporary cases. Other neuropathies that can cause Charcot foot include alcoholic neuropathy, tabes dorsalis (tertiary syphilis), leprosy, syringomyelia, hereditary sensory neuropathies, and post-traumatic or post-spinal cord injury syndromes.

symptomKeywords: one foot warmer than other diabetes
metaTitle: Charcot Foot: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Charcot foot causes painless bone collapse in the neuropathic foot. Symptoms, total contact cast treatment and prevention, medically reviewed.

sources.title: Rogers LC, Frykberg RG, Armstrong DG, et al. 2011. The Charcot foot in diabetes (joint task force ADA/AACE consensus). Diabetes Care 34(9):2123-2129.

Schaper NC, van Netten JJ, Apelqvist J, et al. 2023. IWGDF practical guidelines on the prevention and management of diabetic foot disease. Diabetes Metab Res Rev 39(3):e3657.
Petrova NL, Edmonds ME. 2008. Charcot neuro-osteoarthropathy — current standards. Diabetes Metab Res Rev 24(Suppl 1):S58-61.
Frykberg RG, Belczyk R. 2008. Epidemiology of the Charcot foot. Clin Podiatr Med Surg 25(1):17-28.
Pinzur MS, Lio T, Posner M. 2006. Treatment of Eichenholtz stage I Charcot foot arthropathy with a weightbearing total contact cast. Foot Ankle Int 27(5):324-329.
